Or are they a hybrid of the two? Okay. And so forth. How much of their sales is happening automated online through video sales, right? A lot of people doing that these days. So usually it's a hybrid as well. So I always look at the sales and I also just as much always look at the marketing side of the equation. I say, and how are they going about getting their leads? So number one is I want to see how they actually sound. Forget about what they're saying. I want to see how they sound. Do they sound good? Do they have good tonality? If they're out in the field, are they using the right body language? They don't have salesmen that go out into the field, but they have people that come to them, right? So what they'll do is they have all these advertisements and free giveaways. We'll give you this weekend here or two days free, a comfort as long as you listen to this presentation, right? And then they'll have 50, 100 people a day come into their offices, right? And then I can listen to them that way. But there's all these different variables, right? But I want to see how, number one, how do the salespeople sound, right? Here's the biggest, here's one major takeaway here today for everybody is that the big mistake many of you might be making, hopefully, if you're, if you've been here for a while, you're probably not doing this anymore. But the biggest mistake I see with scripts is that they, people will use scripts, but they're not actually usable scripts. The scripts are flawed in so many ways. And the two big flaws that I see are that they're not actually end-to-end. Their definition of a script will be one language pattern, one good way of saying something, like the first sentence of the introduction, like that's their script. You know, yeah, here's my script. And they'll give you like, it's like this long, or this long, and then there's nothing after there's a looping patterns, there's no, you get it, there's no questions, they don't have that question scripted out for their intelligence gathering. So they think they're using a script, but they're not using, that's not using the script. Okay, using a script means you have everything well thought out, the perfect words written in, in, in the spoken words, so they're actually usable, a flow like butter, you get it. So very often, they're not using scripts. I want to see what they're saying. Okay. And then number three, okay, I want to see how they're handling objections. What do they do when the customers inevitably say, let me think about it, let me call you back? How are they handling all the people who sit on the fence as the phrase goes? Let me tell you something. At the end of the day, your success in sales and in business, by the way, is going to be directly related to your ability to get people who are sitting on the fence, the people who have all the common objections. Let me think about it. Let me call your back, bedtime, your success is going to be based on turning those people into customers. It's not, the lay downs are the lay downs, you get it? The lay downs are easy and every company out there, even if they suck at sales, they still get their lay downs, which is why companies can still make some money, even when their sales kind of suck. How many people, not that these first two aren't important, they're crucial, of course, right? But at the end of the day, what comes down to money is how do you take the people who say, let me think about, what's their strategy for that? Now, is that included in that is, do they work on callbacks? Do they have follow up marketing? Do they have any email? They send to people that way. That's number three. And then number four is what type of referral or affiliate programs that they have in place. How do they take their existence? This is by one of the biggest misses of most companies is that they don't go to their customers they have and get them to buy more. The most obvious thing of all is to go to the people that you're serving right now, that you're giving value to, who you're happy customers, and coming up with other products that they can use, they need that will benefit them and making money. It's a huge miss for most companies. They don't exploit that at all in a good way, right? That's one thing I saw. I look at the sales from sort of, you know, stem to stern, so to speak, right? From top to bottom, right? And just as much as that, I also look at the marketing. Where are they getting their leads from? And are they supplying their sales force with leads? Or are they making it the job of the sales person to go out and get their own leads? I will tell you, I've had a company that I consulted for like a couple hundred salesmen a year and turning over 90% of them. Only 10% would stay. I'm like, what the fuck, right? And they hired me to reduce that attrition rate. And of course, it's very easy to reduce attrition through motivation each day and also by teaching people to sound good, know what to say, all the sales straight line aspect. But do you know that the one of the just as important is that one of the biggest things, they were not providing their salespeople with any leads. So what happened was the sales force was spending like most 80% of their day trying to even figure out who to call. You get it? Once they supply their sales force with a steady stream of leads, not qualified leads, unqualified just leads, people to call the numbers skyrocketed. They skyrocketed. Now at the same time, of course, I also worked on their skills. I spent an awful lot of time teaching them the straight line to sound great, to know what to say. But I would say even more importantly, seriously, because it was just so drastic here, they were getting no leads. So they were just they were making like 20 calls a day versus 200 calls a day. So there's a domino effect. Remember this when you're whether you're in sales or a sales manager, own the company doesn't matter. If you're not supplying your sales force with qualified leads, or even unqualified leads, right, and leaving it up to them, you're making a big error in my judgment, big error, because here's the deal. Very few people who are good at sales are also great at marketing or even have that prowess they can be taught, but they just don't know it until they don't know where to go. Many salesmen are broke when they first get something can't afford to buy leads. The best investment you can make in yourself as a salesman, or in your company, as an owner or as a sales manager in your sales force, right, is to support you. But I've been through that, you know, right. And no, don't want to be dead horse. You get that. Okay. So now that being said, now let's I talked about this, this overarching syntax, right. And again, by the way, guys, you know, my ability to write with my right hand is going to dump. I have to get my shoulder replaced. I'll be right on my lefty. It probably won't be much different. I suck writing right. So it's not like the straight line created those elements. Those are naturally occurring elements in any situation of influence. They must those five quailments. So I call them the five core elements of the straight line system. I'll repeat them. Number one, they must love your product. Number two, they must trust and connect with you, the salesperson definition of action threshold is it is the essentially it's the level of collective certainty that any individual prospect needs to be at before they say yes. In other words, how certain does a person have to be about the product about you and the company before they naturally are inclined to say, yeah, I'll do it. I have a low action threshold meaning yeah, I might be reasonably certain. And I'm sure I'll do it. Yeah, I'll buy it. Why not? I like to buy shit, right? My father, the opposite, he's like, I'm not buying anything unless I'm damn fucking certain, right? I was like, trust the salesman, no. So my father's got a high action threshold. I have a low one, right? It'll lower someone's action threshold if it's too high, because what someone has a high action threshold, what they say is, let me think about it. That's what they that's why the that's why the objections pop up typically, right? So it's got a very high action threshold and they're just not certain enough yet, right? And that last element of five is your pain threshold that essentially people tend to buy quickly when they're feeling pain. And I mean pain like a pitch pain meaning emotional pain, anguish, uncertainty, fear, they're like, they're out of control about something, they want to get back in control. That's what you do when you sell you put them back in control of their lives by giving them a product that solves a pain point. Okay. So those I call them the five core elements of the straight line system. But here's the deal. Whether you're using the straight line or not, they still exist. Right? So what happens is if you're not using the straight line, and you're unaware of them, it takes you many calls until you accidentally line them up. That's the difference. That's why we can collapse time with the straight line, because we're deliberately going after these five core elements in a methodical way, in a way that the best closes in the world do it naturally. So essentially what's a straight line represents and when I hear offense, oh my God, from great sales, and I guys, that's exactly what I do. You just wrote down what I do. Yeah. Exactly. A system is represents what great salesmen do naturally and allows anyone to do that. So let's go through these steps one by one. Okay. And we give you the broader overview today of these steps will dig into them each week. Okay. Number one, one, always the first step in every sale is what you must and everyone should know this by now. You must take immediate control. You must take control of the sale. Why? Because without control, you can't line up anything. You get it there and control there. Because if you want to do things in a predictable fashion, we give them you back the words that actually preceded me inventing this straight line what hit me is I looked at my 12 guys and don't you guys get it? Every sales the same. They're like, what? I'm like, every sales the same watch it's a straight line. And I drew the straight line on the book. What I meant by that was that I as a world class natural salesman, I line up these same elements and every time the same way because I'm in control. So I take control of the sale. I take immediate control. Okay, I'm not going to explain even how to do it today. Because most of you already know we'll reinforce it next week or the week after. But the point is this is about just brought over views right now, right? You take immediate control by, you know, very quickly by sounding sharp as attack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in your field. And that last one an expert in your field. This is the linchpin because people we've been taught since we a be to defer to experts when we are in the process presence of someone we perceive to be an expert deferred we give up control. Okay, so you must take control why? Because that allows you now to line up the elements in a predictable fashion the same way every time. The second step, and this is important is we use this control, not the Baba, Baba, Baba to become a talking head. The mistake will make this Oh, this is when you take control, you want to talk talk talk. No, you want to use that to ask questions, we gather intelligence. We ask a certain set of questions to find out not just if our prospect needs our product, but if they get a foreign product, what their beliefs are, what their values are, what their hierarchy values is, what their pain point is, the main pain point, the secondary pain points, we identify all their major important data that decide a whether they should go down the strait like how we help them and be are they qualified and where they're pain lies, right? And then as we do that, we simultaneously get into massive rapport, right on both a conscious and unconscious level. We build rapport a conscious and unconscious level by engaging in a technique called active listening. And through the use of tonalities when we ask all questions, we use certain tonalities that show that number one, you care. And number two, you're just like them that you understand them, you've used process things the same way you feel their pain. Okay, the certain tonalities that we use that allow us to fall into tight rapport. And then we also have our most importantly, active listening meeting that when we ask a question and someone responds, we're not like uh, uh, well, like, uh, uh, yep, God, we give them the, uh, yes, yeah. So you, you, you converse with them as their, uh, yeah, God makes sense that you finish their sentences. Sometimes you know what they're going to say that shows you're an expert, but most importantly builds massive rapport. And when you're done with this sort of double headed monster of gathering intelligence and building rapport, next step is your straight line transition. This is where you essentially you decide whether or not the person should continue down the straight line. Can you help them? If the answer is yes, you say, well, Jim, based on what you said to me, this is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why. If not, you say, Jim, you know, honestly, I really can't help you. It's not, this is what I have here. It doesn't really, it's not going to help you out. I wish you, you know, great luck. Maybe you can go send them somewhere else, but don't try to sell someone who you can help. It's not ethical. You're wasting your time. Or if they're not interested, just don't, don't force the square peg down there into the round hell. Okay. But assuming they are interested, then next, the next step of the syntax is we make our straight line presentation. And this is where we begin the process, by the way, of lining up those first three core elements that they must love the product, trust and connect with you and trust the company that stands behind the product. And again, we frame, we don't front load, we frame, okay, because we're going to end, we always end our presentation with a few things. So step six is we're going to ask for the order for the first time. We ask for the order. Now on top of that, I want to add a couple of things. Number one, remember whenever possibly want to end with urgency before we ask for the order, and remember your overarching equation of energy in benefits out, meaning that when you frames on your old training is going to the library, I'm not going to repeat it today, right? Just very quickly when you frame a closing scenario, it's very simple to get started. And this you're going to get this benefit, remember, reset your benefits, and you ask for the order and you shut up. Right? That this step six here closes out the front half of the sale. That's it. Front half of the sales closed, right? Now, three things can happen. They could say yes, and they buy one is in a lay down sale and the objections right? B, they can say no, right? I'm not interested. Or three, they could say maybe. Now, yes, that lay down is pretty rare. You don't get them that often when you're selling, but they happen sometimes. Yeah, I wanted they just almost pre sold, right? No is even rarer, by the way, because remember, you would have already weeded out the people who weren't interested in you get it. They weren't interested. You're not you don't want to be presenting to people who don't want your product. You get it. You don't want to do this pointless, right? Maybe is all the common objections that we hear. Think about it, call your back, blah, blah, blah. And for them to quickly go through before we're running out of time here is number seven is we're going to deflect the initial objection, meaning we don't answer it head on deflect the initial objection. I hear what you're saying, but this is the idea makes sense to you. Do you like the idea? Don't worry about the tone out. It's not the right tone out. It was only the latest out. We would deflect the objection. And then what we do is we're going to run our first looping pattern meeting. We essentially now watch, let me draw the straight line out for you here. Watch. This is the straight line, not that straight. My shoulders, I can't even hold my shoulder that long. This is your open. This is your close, right? Is your dotted lines above and below the line, right? The front half of the sale say ends, not really a half, it's usually a third, right? Ends right there, right? When someone says, let me think about it, what that means then not certain yet, not certain enough. So we need to essentially loop back into presentation mode. We go back and we essentially resell them. Remember the first, your present, your initial presentation, your initial preso is about framing, meaning that you don't throw the kitchen fucking sink at them. That's not how you close at a high level. It doesn't work. People can only take so much. People don't go from, imagine the certainty scale, right? Here's a one, here's a 10. This is absolute certainty. They love your prop. This is where they think it's absolute uncertainty, the biggest piece of shit in the world, biggest piece of shit, best thing since sliced bread, right? That's the continuum right there, right? When someone says to you, yeah, it sounds pretty good. That's not a 10. They're like, you say, yeah, hey, do you like that? They say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. It's like a five or a six. So what you need to do is you need to loop back and make them more certain, say things to them, show them documents, sales aids, right? That's what you're doing when you loop back. You are increasing someone's level of certainty. And this is the key guys to closing at a high level is that you essentially untrained salesmen at best, by the way, they think, let me make a great presentation and I just got to pressure someone to buy. That's not what happens. You frame your presentation and you keep representing, representing, representing and each time you tell them more and so they keep saying, let me think, let me call you back, you get them closer and closer to that level of, which is, by the way, where they're okay, step eight, well, now you're looping your goal when you loop is to bring their level of certainty above their action threshold. So when you ask for the, so here you get a loop once, right? And you run this loop and again, we're going to dig into this in the following. So I'm not going to get specific here, right? Every loop always ends with you asking for the order. Each time you ask for the order, they can do what they could say yes, no, or maybe. Well, again, Noah's pretty much adding the equation. So it's like yes, or another objection. If they say, yes, you have a closed sale and you're done. If they say another objection, what you do very simply is you're going to loop back again. So every time they hit your objection, you're looping back and representing strengthening your case, making them more and more certain. You get it on both a logical and an emotional. There's two types of certainty. Hopefully you know that by now. If you don't, that's another subject we'll deal with in the following weeks to come, right? But remember, you keep looping back and looping back. Now, in terms of the actual syntax, watch, after this first loop on the second, so you're going to keep running every loop ends with you asking for the order. If they don't say yes, they give you another objection, you're going to run another loop, except this time you're going to lower their action threshold. There's a pattern that we use. I'm not going to get into it right now because it's in the old trains and I'll dig into it next week. That's probably what I'll do next week, right? You're going to lower their action threshold. And again, you're going to always end the loop of asking for the order. Then next loop, number 10 is going to be, you're going to add on pain. And again, this is pain as the part of your loop and end with asking for the order. So every loop always ends with you asking for the order. In this loop, you insert the action threshold lowering strategy. In this loop, you, this loop, use the action threshold strategy. In this next loop, loop in the three is your pain addition strategy. And then after that, you can keep running loop at the old buy. You can keep going and going and going. But again, that's a matter of how many loops you want to run, separate issue. The point is, now we get to step 11, which is, right, so we're going to put in how many loops that you ran, right? Where either you close the deal or you end the call. They didn't buy it. There's a certain number of loops, right? Now I'll get into the subject of callbacks. And if you want to set up a callback, separate issue here, we're going to go to the syntax here. And if you close your, is we end the call and buy for you. But if they bought now, you end, go to customers for life strategies. This is where you're going to add on your strategies now for getting referrals for getting repeat buys from the same customer. So there's a whole new set of strategies that kick in and step 12. If they buy, now we go into phase two of the straight line, which is developing customers for life. How do you get them to buy from you again and again and again, turn a one time buy into a long term raving fan who gives you a massive number of referrals. That's the syntax of the straight line eyes. What the syntax allows you to do is essentially bring order to what most people in sales, they see as absolute chaos. They don't know what comes first, what comes second, by taking control, you allow yourself to systematically pick the lock, so to speak, on their buying strategy. Imagine each number is in is one part of a combination lock to a safe. Get all five numbers, right? The safe opens when you ask for the order, they buy. If one number is wrong, they're not certain enough that it's actually those two line, no pain, they don't buy, the safe doesn't open with cracking the code of someone's buying strategy. There's something missing there. That's what step four is. Step four is simply a transition where you have finished gathering intelligence and you've done it in the way they built rapport and you now say to them, these very simple words, Jim, Jill, Juan, John, James, based on what you just said to me, mean I asked you questions, you gave me answers, based on what you said to me, this product is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why you lower your voice, right? Now comes your next step, step five, which is your straight line presentation. This is where you actually offer the solution. You can explain, here's my product. This is why it's awesome. This is why it's going to resolve your pain. This is why it's going to fucking do everything. It's the best one out there. All you're doing is framing, explaining the basics logically, why your product makes sense for them, why it's the best deal out there, it solves all their problems. That's the best explanation I can give. I think that doesn't next question. Next question is from Landon. Can you give an example of questions or dialogue for creating emotional certain? Sure. So remember, logical certainty is based on the words that you say. In other words, where you say my product does this, it's a cost benefit ratio. It's better than the competition. Here's what it costs. Here are the benefits that you get. It's very arithmetic. So when someone is logically certain, they can connect all the dots from front to back and say, yep, this makes fucking sense. The product is awesome. The TV sets, the best TV set out there, the picture quality is amazing. It's easy to use. It looks great on my wall. It's got a three-year warranty and the brand is impeccable. It's the third. The company is a great Samsung, a great company, or LG, right? That's logical certainty. It looks great, seems great, great price. Does everything, it's all the smart features I want. It's gorgeous to look at. And emotional certainty is I can imagine how great I'll feel when the family and I are sitting around on a Sunday watching a fucking movie and bonding and building memories for a lifetime. How great is that going to be? It's going to be an object for my family and I to create wonderful memories around this TV. Do you see the difference? In other words, one of them is an imagine myself in the future using the product and enjoying it and feeling good. I imagine my friends coming over and saying, wow, how beautiful does that look? What an awesome TV. We watch football and we're bonding. There's men or women and we just feel great and the family kids love it. They're playing their games on there and they're happy and the family dog spot is there. That's emotional certainty where someone use, they see themselves in the future using the product and feeling good. The car is got this horsepower, that it's a great price. It's beautiful to look at it. It's good. It's got a great brand and imagine how good I'm going to feel driving this with the top down along the Pacific Coast Highway with the wind in my hair feeling fucking powerful and sexy. That's emotional certainty. People don't buy on logic. They buy an emotion. They justify the decision with logic. They got to have both. There you go. Next question. Yeah.
